turns.content: I'm new to open source contributions and I know how to build a React component, but this Rust backend feels completely different. Can someone explain what Axum is and why it's used instead of standard HTTP libraries?

Axum acts like the router for your web service by wrapping tower services into axum routes, but think of it as a bridge that connects high-level handlers to low-level middleware. Imagine you are building a highway system where Axum is the traffic control center that directs cars (requests) to specific lanes (handlers). For tracing specifically, you would integrate tower_http::TraceLayer which adds span per request automatically so every incoming car gets logged with its route and status code.

That highway analogy helps me visualize the flow, but I'm confused about how exactly the TraceLayer fits into my axum router setup. Does it replace the standard tower middleware or does it sit alongside other components like error handling and logging?

The trace layer sits on top of your existing tower stack without replacing core functionality, so you can keep your custom make_span function to inject tracing spans before the request reaches your axum handlers. You would configure it by calling make_service_with_make_span on your service builder, which wraps every handler in a span that records status codes and timing, ensuring your observability pipeline captures every interaction without breaking your existing error handling logic.

I see how the make_span function customizes the span creation, but I'm struggling to understand how this connects to my frontend React state management and why Rust's tracing system is better suited for backend observability compared to what we use in JavaScript frameworks.

Your React components manage local UI state, but the trace layer handles global request lifecycle events across the entire service, which requires a centralized logging model that Rust provides through its strong type system and safe concurrency guarantees. While JavaScript frameworks often rely on async/await patterns for tracing, tower_http::TraceLayer offers a synchronous, type-safe pipeline where every span is explicitly constructed via make_span and recorded with precise timestamps, making it easier to debug distributed systems without the overhead of event loop complexity.

I'm trying to get observability into my Rust web service like I have in Node.js with middlewares, but I keep thinking about the trace layer and span per request. It feels exactly like adding a middleware chain in Express where each handler logs its response status code. I just want to record every incoming HTTP request's status code automatically without writing manual logging for each route.

You're describing tower_http::TraceLayer which is the standard way to wrap your router so that every request gets a span and you can log its status code. The TraceLayer wraps your router to create spans for each incoming request, recording the HTTP status codes as you requested. You configure it by passing an instance of this type to axum's service function so the tracing infrastructure attaches itself automatically.

That sounds perfect until I try it and my spans are empty or missing the status code entirely. It feels like I'm doing something wrong with how I initialize the layer because the output isn't matching what I expect from a simple middleware stack.

The common mistake is forgetting to use custom make_span when your application requires specific span metadata. When you define a custom make_span function, it allows you to inject extra information like the user's IP or request path into every span generated by tower_http::TraceLayer. If you skip this step and rely on defaults, the spans might not contain the fields you need for meaningful tracing.

Wait, so if I just want to record status codes without extra metadata, do I still need that make_span function? That seems like overkill because my Node.js equivalent doesn't require custom logic at all.

You don't strictly need a complex make_span for just recording status codes, but the concept is what makes Rust observability flexible. The why behind this design pattern is that tower_http::TraceLayer is built around composition where you can layer different tracing strategies without rewriting your application. If you only care about status codes, a simple implementation of make_span that captures the response code works perfectly fine.

Okay so I'll just write a tiny function to extract the status and pass it into the layer configuration. But why does Rust force me to think about this at the service level instead of just wrapping my routes like in Python or Node.js?

Because axum's architecture requires the service function to receive the router and any global middleware configurations upfront. This forces you to define your tracing strategy once for all requests rather than patching individual routes, which aligns with how tower_http::TraceLayer integrates into the request pipeline. The benefit is that every span gets consistent metadata across your entire application without needing per-route logic.

I've been reading about tower_http::TraceLayer and span per request but I still don't get how it actually works in a real app. It feels too abstract to me, like I'm missing something fundamental about why we need this layer.

The core idea is that tower_http::TraceLayer wraps your service and intercepts every HTTP request before it reaches the actual handler. Instead of manually wrapping logic, you attach a single instance of TraceLayer to your router using .layer(). This layer automatically generates a span for each incoming request, recording metadata like the status code returned by the handler into the trace context. You don't need to write boilerplate code because the library handles the propagation of these spans through the middleware stack.

Okay, so I just attach it once? But what if I want custom logic like extracting headers or handling specific error paths differently? The documentation mentions make_span but I can't see how to customize that without breaking the flow.

You define a closure for make_span that receives the span builder and returns a new Span. Inside this closure, you can read headers from the request using .headers() or inspect the response status code before it's finalized. You might check if the path matches a specific route to conditionally add tags like 'route_type' or 'custom_header'. The key is that make_span is called for every span created by the layer, so you get full control over what data gets recorded while keeping the integration transparent to your application logic.
